topic: Someone receives a package intended for the previous tenant
raw_output:
[story 1]

The cardboard box sat on my doorstep like an unwelcome monument. A crisp, unfamiliar label addressed to 'Apartment 3B,' the unit I had vacated three weeks prior. My fingers, cold despite the mild afternoon, traced the faded ink. A tightness began to coil in my chest, a dull, heavy pressure that made it hard to draw a full breath. I stared at it, my gaze flickering from the box to the worn welcome mat, a knot forming just beneath my ribs. I knew, with a sinking certainty, that this was someone else's story now, and I was the unwilling custodian of their lingering narrative. I hesitated, the urge to simply discard it warring with a strange, paralyzing need to examine the contents.

[story 2]

I found myself hovering by the mail slot for an hour, my shoulders hunched inward as if trying to physically shrink away from the world. When I finally retrieved the package, the weight of it felt disproportionate, a leaden anchor in my hand. The paper was thick, sealed with heavy-duty tape. My palms grew clammy as I unfolded the label, my eyes scanning the handwriting—a script I didn't recognize. A tremor ran up my arm, a tiny vibration that seemed to echo the unsettled feeling churning inside me. I took a slow, shallow breath, trying to steady the quickening rhythm of my pulse. What was inside? The anticipation felt like a physical ache behind my eyes.

[story 3]

When the delivery person handed over the parcel, the brief interaction felt jarring. I accepted the box with a stiff nod, my expression carefully neutral, though I could feel the involuntary clenching in my jaw. Walking back to my door, the silence of the hallway amplified the thumping of my own heart. I set the package down on the small entryway table, avoiding direct eye contact with the object. Every nerve ending seemed acutely aware of the intrusion—a message from a life that was no longer mine. I paced a small circuit, my movements jerky and restless, unable to settle until I could get closer, to assess the extent of this unexpected handover.
